epigraph.text: By their fruit you will recognize them.
epigraph.quote: The Residue Theorem is the most useful theorem in complex analysis.

content.text:
The Residue Theorem unifies all our work on contour integration. It 
expresses any closed contour integral in terms of residues.

content.title: Residue Theorem
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic inside and on a simple closed contour $\gamma$, 
except for isolated singularities $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n$ inside $\gamma$. 
Then
\[
 \oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Res_{z_k}(f).
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Surround each $z_k$ with a small circle $C_k$. By the deformation principle:
\[
 \oint_\gamma f = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \oint_{C_k} f.
\]
Each $\oint_{C_k} f = 2\pi i \cdot \Res_{z_k}(f)$ by the definition of 
residue.

content.solution:
The integrand has simple poles at $z = 1$ and $z = -1$, both inside $|z| = 2$.

$\Res_1\left(\frac{1}{z^2-1}\right) = \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{z-1}{z^2-1} = \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{1}{z+1} = \frac{1}{2}$.

$\Res_{-1}\left(\frac{1}{z^2-1}\right) = \lim_{z \to -1}\frac{z+1}{z^2-1} = \lim_{z \to -1}\frac{1}{z-1} = -\frac{1}{2}$.

By the Residue Theorem:
\[
 \oint_{|z|=2} \frac{dz}{z^2 - 1} = 2\pi i\left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right) = 0.
\]

Singularities: simple pole at $z = 0$, pole of order 2 at $z = 1$.

At $z = 0$: $\Res_0 = \lim_{z \to 0} z \cdot \frac{e^z}{z(z-1)^2} = \frac{e^0}{(-1)^2} = 1$.

At $z = 1$: $\Res_1 = \frac{d}{dz}\left[(z-1)^2 \cdot \frac{e^z}{z(z-1)^2}\right]_{z=1} = \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{e^z}{z}\right]_{z=1}$.

$\frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{e^z}{z}\right) = \frac{ze^z - e^z}{z^2} = \frac{e^z(z-1)}{z^2}$.

At $z = 1$: $\frac{e^1(0)}{1} = 0$.

Thus $\oint_{|z|=2} \frac{e^z}{z(z-1)^2}\,dz = 2\pi i(1 + 0) = 2\pi i$.

marginNotes.text:
For just as the body is one and 
has many members, and all the members of the body, though many, are one 
body, so it is with Christ.

marginNotes.author: Lars Ahlfors
marginNotes.text:
The residue theorem reduces integration to 
algebra.
